coredata.dc:title: In-situ synthesis of carbon dot at cellulose nanofiber for durable water treatment membrane with high selectivity
coredata.dc:creator: Ahn, Jungbin
Pak, Sewon
Song, Younghan
Kim, Hyungsup
coredata.dc:description: In this work, carbon dot (CD) was in-situ synthesized and attached to cellulose nanofiber (CNF) via hydrothermal process. The in-situ synthesized CD uniformly enveloped the CNF surface by means of amide bonding, without significant changes of the chemical structure of CNF. The prepared CD@CNF composite showed rough and bumpy morphology. The attached CD increased the interaction between the fibers and enhanced the thermal stability and the dimensional stability in aqueous solution. CD@CNF showed excellent performance as a dye-rejection membrane with high-water flux (∼32 LMH bar−1) and high rejection rate (∼99.8 %), as well as the selective removal of cationic dye. This study suggests a novel synthesizing method of durable CNF membrane by envelopment of CD for effective water treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot (CD)
In-situ synthesis
CD@CNF membrane
Morphological durability
Water treatment

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In-situ synthesis of CD at CNF (CD@CNF)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Amide bonding formation mechanism between the CNF and the CDs during the in-situ synthesis.
TEM images of CD@CNF (a) with low magnification, (b) with high magnification, (c) CNF, (d) CD and (e) SAXS profiles of CNF and CD@CNF suspensions. (the inset graph shows the Guinier plots).
XRD profiles of CD, CNF and CD@CNF.
(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) high-resolution XPS spectra of CD, CNF, CD-CNF blend and CD@CNF.
(a) UV absorption spectra of the samples and PL emission spectra of (b) CD, (c) CD-CNF and (d) CD@CNF suspensions.
DTG curves of CD, CNF, CD-CNF and CD@CNF.
(a) Water contact angle of CNF, CD-CNF and CD@CNF films. Digital images of (b) CNF (c) CD-CNF blend and (d) CD@CNF film immersed in distilled water for 1 month.

(a) Pure water flux of CD@CNF (black) and CNF (red) membranes according to the loading amounts. MB rejection rate of CD@CNF membrane at each loading amounts (b) reusability test of CD@CNF membrane for MB rejection and (c) comparison of CNF and CD@CNF on selective dye removal performance in the presence of cationic, anionic and neutral dyes (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Dye rejection performance of CD@CNF membrane (20 gsm) on differently charged dyes (10 ppm).

head.ce:title: In-situ synthesis of carbon dot at cellulose nanofiber for durable water treatment membrane with high selectivity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic and Nano System Engineering, Konkuk University, Seoul, 05029, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Organic and Nano System Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Organic and Nano System Engineering, Konkuk University, Seoul, 05029, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, carbon dot (CD) was in-situ synthesized and attached to cellulose nanofiber (CNF) via hydrothermal process. The in-situ synthesized CD uniformly enveloped the CNF surface by means of amide bonding, without significant changes of the chemical structure of CNF. The prepared CD@CNF composite showed rough and bumpy morphology. The attached CD increased the interaction between the fibers and enhanced the thermal stability and the dimensional stability in aqueous solution. CD@CNF showed excellent performance as a dye-rejection membrane with high-water flux (∼32 LMH bar−1) and high rejection rate (∼99.8 %), as well as the selective removal of cationic dye. This study suggests a novel synthesizing method of durable CNF membrane by envelopment of CD for effective water treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Cellulose, the most abundant natural polymer on earth, has increasing attention for its outstanding benefits such as biodegradability, sustainability, mechanical strength and solvent resistance. Recently, nano-celluloses including nano-fibril cellulose and cellulose nanocrystal have been widely studied in various application areas. Tempo (2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxylradical) oxidization of cellulose is one of the most common methods to fibrillate cellulose in nanoscale (Isogai, Saito, & Fukuzumi, 2011). Tempo-oxidized cellulose nanofiber (CNF) has carboxylate groups, which give CNF high chemical reactivity. CNF have extremely high aspect ratio, high specific surface area and improved mechanical strength, as well. Using those features, CNF is fabricated in the forms of aerogel, hydrogel or film to apply for contaminant absorber, effective catalyst, substrate for devices, and so on (Chen, Chen, Wang, Yao, & Wang, 2017; Gao et al., 2019; He et al., 2014). Among them, membrane for water treatment is one of the most potential application areas because its specific characteristics meet the social demands.

Conventionally, water treatment membrane has been fabricated using synthetic polymers such as polysulfone, polyethersulfone of polyvinylidene fluoride (Zeman & Zydney, 2017). To control the porous structure, non-solvent induced phase separation technology is carried out during the fabrication of synthetic polymeric membranes. Although the process develops asymmetric pore structure for high-performance water treatment, the overuse of hazard solvents like dimethylformamide (DMF) and dimethylacetamide (DMAc) is inevitable, resulting in paradoxical hazard on earth (Figoli et al., 2014). In addition, those polymers are one of the most lethal pollutes when they are discarded. In order to protect the earth from those kinds of pollutions, novel materials and synthesis methods for the membrane are critical. From those points of view, CNF is a substantial candidate for water treatment membrane. The negatively charged carboxylate groups and the modified functional groups on the CNF surface are highly effective to capture contaminants with various sizes from molecules to particles (Jia, Zhang, Feng, Zhou, & Yao, 2020; Zhang, Uchikoshi, Ichinose, & Liu, 2019).

A large number of studies have been reported to achieve high performance (durability, high flux and high rejection rate) by utilizing the advantages of CNF (Park, Shin, Cheng, & Hyun, 2017; Voisin, Bergström, Liu, & Mathew, 2017; Zhan et al., 2018). In the early stages, CNF was used as an ultrathin active layer on a porous substrate (Xiong et al., 2017). Due to the nano-order thickness, the membrane showed low mechanical strength and low rejection rate below 95 %, which did not meet the requirement of the practical use. It was very challenging to increase the thickness of the CNF membrane. When the CNF was overlaid thick, the fibers were steadily stacked and reduced the pore size consequently. To maintain the porous structure without compaction, nanoparticle was introduced as a spacer. Varanasi et al. improved the water flux up to 80 Lm−2 h-1 bar-1 by embedding silica nanoparticle in CNF membrane (Varanasi, Low, & Batchelor, 2015). Due to the low interaction between the nanoparticle and CNF, the additional cross-linking agent was required. To improve the stable fixation of nanoparticle in CNF matrix without scarification of the membrane performance, nanoparticle having high affinity toward CNF is needed.

Carbon dot (CD) is one of the attractive and noticeable carbon nanomaterials, owing to easy synthesis procedure. As widely known, the chemical structure of CD can be easily tuned by controlling the synthetic conditions (Ahn, Song, Kwon, Lee et al., 2019; Woo, Song, Ahn, & Kim, 2020). With its tunable functional groups, CD have high affinity to various polymers including CNF. There are only limited number of researches on embedding CDs in CNF matrix by means of either physical or chemical way (Chen et al., 2020; Quraishi, Plappert, Grießer, Gindl-Altmutter, & Liebner, 2019; Xue et al., 2020). However, most of the studies focused on the luminescence properties of the CD, or the enhanced mechanical strength of the composite.

In this work, the high-performance water treatment membrane was synthesized by in-situ synthesis of CD on the surface of CNF. Without using any organic solvents, the CD homogeneously enveloped the CNF surface via amide bonding and made the surface rougher and bumpier. As well, the CD decreased the repulsive force between the CNFs. The resulted membrane was structurally durable in water, and exhibited high-water flux over 32 LMH bar−1. Compared to the CNF membrane, the CD@CNF membrane showed excellent selectivity on cationic dye rejection over 99 %, even in the presence of anionic and neutral dyes. The CD@CNF also had re-usability, showing over 95 % of dye rejection after the third recycling cycle.

Tempo-oxidized cellulose nanofiber (CNF) suspension (1.8 wt% in water, carboxylate content: 1.7 mmol g−1) was purchased from ANPOLY (Republic of Korea). During the CNF synthesis procedure, cellulose was sufficiently purified to remove hemicellulose and lignin. The precursors of CD (glutathione (GT) and citric acid (CA)), and the dyes (Methylene Blue (MB, 82 % dye content) and Orange G (OG, 80 % dye content), Vitamin B12 (VB12, 98 % dye content), Methyl Orange (MO, 85 % dye content) and Janus Green B (JGB, 65 % dye content)) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (USA). All chemicals were used without any treatment.

The CD in this study was prepared via hydrothermal carbonization of GT and CA. Briefly, 0.5 mmol of GT and CA were dissolved in 30 mL distilled water. For the synthesis, the solution was kept at 120 °C for 4 h in a Teflon-lined autoclave. After the hydrothermal reaction, the obtained mixture was purified by dialysis using a membrane (0.5−1 kDa, Biotech CD Tubing) against water for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ synthesis of CD at CNF (CD@CNF)
ce:section.ce:para: The CD was synthesized at CNF, similarly as the CD synthesis. 0.5 mmol of GT and CA were carefully dissolved in 35.5 g of CNF suspension. The suspension was poured in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 4 h to in-situ synthesize the CD at CNF. The in-situ synthesized CD at CNF was named as CD@CNF. For the comparison, the pre-synthesized CD was mixed with the CNF by a 1:1 ratio based on the weight percent and named as CD-CNF blend.

The morphologies of the prepared samples were observed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEM-F200, JEOL, Japan) operating at 80 kV. For TEM sample preparation, each CNF suspension was diluted to 0.09 wt%. The CD suspension concentration was adjusted to 10 mg/mL. The suspensions were vigorously stirred over 4 h. The TEM grid (carbon grid, Woomyoung, Republic of Korea) was UV/ozone (UVO) treated prior to sample deposition. 20 μL of the suspensions were dropped onto the TEM grid and dried at RT for 3 days. To confirm the envelopment of the CD on the CNF surface, AFM images of CNF and CD@CNF were obtained using 0.01 wt% suspensions (XE-70, Park Systems, Korea). The fiber diameter changes by the process were studied using a small-angle X-ray scattering spectrometer (XEUSS 2.0, Xenocs, France). The suspensions were prepared with concentration of 0.09 wt% and the sample to detection distance for the test was 2.5 m. The optical properties of the CD and the CD embedded suspensions were studied using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy (Cary 60 UV/vis spectrophotometer, Agilent Technologies, USA) and PL spectroscopy (FS-2, SICNCO, Republic of Korea).

The prepared CNF suspensions were filtered by nylon filter paper (0.45 μm) for film-formation. The basis weight of the films was kept as 10 gsm (grammage, g m−2). The crystalline structures of each CNF film were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu Kα (λ = 0.154 nm) (D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT/IR-4000, JASCO, UK) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (K-alpha, Thermo Scientific, UK) were used to characterize the chemical structures of the samples. The thermal degradation behaviors of the samples were analyzed using thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA 8000, PerkinElmer, USA).

The pure water flux of the CD@CNF composite membrane was evaluated by filtering deionized water through the membrane which was produced by dead-end filtration (Amicon 8050, Milipore, USA), on filter paper (filter paper No.6, Advantec, Japan). The basis weight of the membrane was controlled from 10 to 30 gsm. The operating water pressure was fixed as 1 bar and the water flux was calculated using the following equation where the effective permeation area (A) is 13.4 cm2. The volume change (ΔV) stands for the collected permeate amount at the interval time (Δt).
J w (LMH = L m−2 h−1) = ΔV∙A−1∙Δt

The SEM images of the membrane were observed using scanning electron microscope (SU8010, Hitachi, Japan). The UV–vis spectroscopy was used to evaluate the rejection of organic dye. Organic solutions (10 ppm of MB or MB/OG/VB12 mixture) were filtered by the prepared membrane and the absorbance difference between the feed solution and the permeate was calculated.

To study the morphologies of the CD grown on the CNF, the TEM images of CD@CNF, the CNF and the CD were obtained (Fig. 1 ). CD@CNF had an entangled fibrous structure as similar to the pristine CNF (Fig. 1 (a) and (c)). The processing conditions for in-situ synthesis did not significantly affect the CNF morphology. The spherical shape of the CD was clearly observed on the CNF surface in the high magnification image (Fig. 1 (b)). In the image, the CD was successfully developed on the CNF surface without obvious aggregation. The size of the CD on the CNF was estimated around 3 nm and the graphitic lattice structure of CD was not apparently observed. To study the morphological change of CD by the CNF, CD was synthesized without CNF under the same condition as CD@CNF (Fig. 1 (d)). The CD showed similar morphological features as the CD on the CNF. It had a spherical shape with the size distribution in 2∼6 nm and a weak graphitic carbon structure. The processing temperature was not high enough to develop the graphitic core structure. The precursors were hard to reconstruct a highly ordered graphitic structure. As well, the fibrous structure of the CNF was not significantly changed during the process. AFM images were also obtained to analyze the CD envelopment on the CNF surface (Fig.1 of (Ahn, Pak, & Kim, 2020)). Both the CNF and CD@CNF had fibrous structures as confirmed by the TEM images. Unlikely to the CNF, CD@CNF had slightly increased height owing to the homogeneous envelopment of the nanomaterials on the fibers surface. In turn, the attachment of the CD on the CNF surface was confirmed again.

SAXS analysis was conducted to study the changes of the fiber diameters by the process. The SAXS profiles and the Guinier plots of the pristine CNF and CD@CNF were shown in Fig. 1 (e). The fiber diameters of each sample were calculated as the radius of gyration (Rg) using the Guinier approximation (Zheng & Best, 2018). The Rg of the pristine CNF was calculated as 64.06 Å, while the fiber of CD@CNF had 64.49 Å. The change might be originated from the two different effects of the process; one is the hydrolysis of CNF and the other is the envelopment of CD on the CNF. As well known, acids such as levulinic acid and formic acid were generated during the hydrothermal treatment of glucose unit (Möller, Harnisch, & Schröder, 2013). The generated acids accelerated the hydrolysis of the CNF, resulting in a decrease of fiber diameter. On contrary, the synthesized CD was enveloped on the CNF and increased the fiber diameter.

Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of the prepared samples. The CD synthesized without CNF had a broad peak around 20°, which was consistent with the (002) lattice spacing of graphite. The d-spacing of the synthesized CD was calculated as 4.2 Å, using Bragg’s Equation, while the d-spacing of bulk graphite is known as 3.3 Å (Srivastava et al., 2012). Compared to bulk graphite, the d-spacing of the synthesized CD was larger due to its poor graphitic crystallization, as already observed in the TEM image (Fig. 1 (d)). Considering the relatively low processing temperature, the CD had less chance to form an ordered graphite structure during the hydrothermal carbonization.

The XRD patterns of the pristine CNF showed cellulose I crystal structure (Park, Baker, Himmel, Parilla, & Johnson, 2010). Even after the in-situ synthesis process, the CD@CNF in the study had typical cellulose I crystal structure as like the pristine CNF. Meanwhile, the characteristic peaks of the CD were not observed at all measured spots. It can be explained in terms of the small quantity and the homogeneous dispersion of the developed CD. The total amount of CD grown on the CNF was too small to influence on the XRD pattern. The CD was also homogeneously dispersed on the CNF without significant aggregation. The crystallinity of the pristine CNF in this study was calculated as 42.67 %, using XRD deconvolution method (Park et al., 2010). After the in-situ synthesis, the crystallinity of the CNF was dramatically increased as 79.7 %. As mentioned above, the CNF was hydrolyzed and decomposed during the hydrothermal treatment (Mohamed, Rahman, Zain, & Wong, 2020; Sevilla & Fuertes, 2009). The amorphous region was more easily and quickly removed compared to the crystalline region, resulting in the increase of crystallinity. CNF has a glucuronate group, in which the hydroxyl group is substituted with a carboxylate group, through Tempo-oxidization process. The glucuronate group is less stable than the glucose group and can be decomposed by mild hydrothermal carbonization. During the in-situ process, the acidic condition accelerated the degradation and hydrolysis of the CNF. Consequently, the CNF of CD@CNF showed higher crystallinity compared to the pristine CNF. Even after the hydrothermal treatment, the crystal structure of cellulose remained as cellulose I.

The FT-IR spectra of the CD, the CNF, the CD-embedded films (CD-CNF blend and CD@CNF) were obtained, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). In the FT-IR spectrum of the CD, the characteristic absorption of OH and NH stretching vibrations were observed around 3400 cm−1 (Ahn, Song, Kwon, Woo, & Kim, 2019). CN stretching vibrations were also observed at 1400 cm−1 (Ahn, Song, Kwon, Woo et al., 2019). The peaks at 1714 cm−1, 1670 cm−1, and 1108 cm−1 are assigned to carboxyl, amide and amine groups (Chen et al., 2018; Mishra, Tripathi, Choudhary, & Gupta, 2015; Ruiz, Cristobal, Laurenti, Retama, & Lopez-Cabarcos, 2014). The peaks in the range of 1000−1400 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO, CS and CH (Ding, Wei, & Xiong, 2014). All results indicate nitrogen and sulfur were successfully doped or functionalized the surface of the CDs.

The FT-IR spectra of the pristine CNF and the CD-embedded films were analyzed and compared to confirm the binding states of the CD to the CNF. The FT-IR spectrum of the pristine CNF film shows the fingerprint peaks of cellulose (1430–850 cm−1) and a peak at 1606 cm−1, denoting as carboxylate group (Garside & Wyeth, 2003; Oun & Rhim, 2017). As mentioned, the hydroxyl group of the CNF was substituted into carboxylate group via Tempo-oxidization process. Likewise, both the CD embedded CNF films had a fingerprint region of cellulose. However, the chemical state of the glucuronate groups (peaks at 1606 cm-1) was changed by introducing the CD in the both cases. The peaks for the carboxylate group were dramatically decreased, indicating the change of the ionic state of the carboxylate group. Meanwhile, the peaks at 1719 cm−1 appeared in both the CD-embedded films, which might be originated from the functional groups of the CD, such as carboxyl and amine groups. The peak corresponding to the CO bonding of amide group at 1604 cm−1 was observed only in the CD@CNF film, while the peak was not observed in the pristine CNF or the CD-CNF blended film (Gong et al., 2014). It indicates that the CD in CD@CNF was chemically bonded to the CNF by amide groups, during the in-situ synthesis. To deeply analyze the amide bonding formation between the CNF and the CD, HTC time was controlled for 1, 2.5 and 4 h for both CD and CD@CNF syntheses (Fig. 2 of (Ahn et al., 2020)). In the FT-IR spectra of the CDs, the peaks at 1400−1000 cm-1 was not changed while the peaks at 1714−1650 cm-1 increased. The core state of the CD was mostly constructed in 1 h and thereafter the functional groups such as carboxyl or amide groups were developed. In the FT-IR spectra of the CNFs, the fingerprint region of cellulose did not change as discussed (Wang, Zhai, Zhu, Li, & Zeng, 2018). However, the peaks at 1719 cm-1 and 1604 cm-1 increased, meanwhile the peak of COO− decreased. As same to the CD, GT and CA constructed the core state and then develop the functional groups such as carboxyl or amide groups. During the functional group formation, the amide linkage between the CD and the CNF were generated. Scheme 1 clearly shows the decomposition-reconstruction mechanism of the CD anchored to the CNF.

To further study the chemical structures, XPS analysis was conducted. Fig. 3 (b) shows the high-resolution spectra of C1s, O1s, S2p, and N1s for each sample. The survey spectra of the samples are displayed in Data in Brief (Fig. 3 of (Ahn et al., 2020)) in Data. The survey spectrum of the CD was composed of C1s, O1s, S2p, and N1s. As confirmed in the FT-IR, the CD had various chemical bonding such as CO, CO, CN, CN and CS, as shown in C1s and O1s spectra (Sun et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2013). As shown in the S2p spectrum, most of the sulfur was bonded with carbon in form of sulfide group (Sun et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2013). Amino and graphitic N bondings were also observed in the N1s spectrum (Sun et al., 2013). As described in the FT-IR, nitrogen and sulfur were doped in the forms of graphitic N and sulfide. At the same time, they were functionalized in the surface as amine and sulfide groups. For the pristine CNF, the survey spectrum was composed of C1s, O1s, Na1s and Cl2p peaks. As described in the FT-IR, the carboxylate group of the CNF was ionically bonded with Na+ in the CNF. The C1s spectrum of the CNF was composed of CC, COC/COH and COO− bindings (Zhu, Soldatov, & Mathew, 2017). The O1s spectrum of the pristine CNF was deconvoluted with CO and CO bonding as expected (Zhu et al., 2017).

After the CD was physically blended with the CNF, Na+ peak disappeared in the survey spectrum. It indicates the ionic bonding between the carboxylate group and Na+ was changed by blending the CD, as discussed in the FT-IR. The chemical changes in the CNF were also observed in its C1s and the O1s spectra. The C1s spectrum of the CD-CNF blended film was deconvoluted with three peaks. Compared to the pristine CNF, the peak of the highest binding energy (COO−) shifted toward higher binding energy of carboxyl group (Pletincx et al., 2017). The carboxylate group was protonated to the carboxyl group by introducing CD. The O1s spectrum showed a similar trend. The O1s spectrum was deconvoluted into two peaks. The peak of CO was diminished, while a new peak of COOH was observed. The change in the chemical states of the blended CD was observed in the S2p spectrum. The sulfur mainly existed in the form of sulfide in the CD. However, the sulfonate group was newly observed in the blended sample (Sun et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2013). By the solidification, the sulfide groups were oxidized in the presence of the CNF. The N1s spectrum of the CD-CNF blended film showed the amino and graphitic N, as coincident with the CD.

Another change was observed in the in-situ synthesized film. The survey spectrum of CD@CNF had C1s, O1s, N1s and S2p peaks. No peak related to Na+ was observed, as like the CD-CNF blended film. This shows that the carboxylate group of the pristine CNF was chemically changed during the in-situ process. CD@CNF exhibited different chemical bindings compared to the CNF and the CD-CNF blended film, in the C1s and O1s. The C1s spectrum of CD@CNF was deconvoluted into CC/CC, COC, CO and COOH. During the in-situ synthesis, the carboxylate group was protonated to carboxyl groups, as like the CD-CNF blended film. The CO bonding was observed, which was inferred as amide bonding from the FT-IR. Similar changes were shown in the O1s spectrum. The O1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks of CO, CO and COOH. Based on the observation in the S2p and N1s spectra, the chemical state of the CD in CD@CNF was compared with the CD in the CD-CNF blended film. In the S2p spectrum, both sulfide and sulfonate groups were observed as like the CD-CNF blended film. Also, amino and graphitic N were confirmed in the N1s spectrum of CD@CNF. The analyses in the FT-IR and the XPS showed insignificant difference in the chemical structures of the CD in both the films. However, the CDs in the films were anchored to the CNF in different ways. The CD in the CD@CNF film was fixed to the CNF with amide bonding, while the CD in the CD-CNF blended film was physically attached to the CNF without chemical bonding.

The physio-chemical states of the CD gave significant influence on the optical properties of the CDs. As shown in the UV absorption spectra of the samples (Fig. 4 (a)), the CD suspension did not exhibit an apparent absorption peak originated from π-π* transition, due to the low graphitization of the CD. However, the strong absorption peak at 330 nm and the shoulder peak up to 420 nm were distinctly observed. They were originated from n-π * transition generated from CO or CN, and the functional groups of the CD, respectively. In similar to the CD, the CD-CNF blended suspension had a small shoulder peak around 330 nm. It was also originated from n- π* transition in the blended CD. However, the broad absorption around 420 nm was negligible because the electrostatic interaction between the functional groups of the CD and the carboxylate groups of the CNF interfered the energy absorption of 420 nm. The CD-CNF blended suspension had a smooth decreasing absorption without apparent peaks. In the high wavelength region, the CD-CNF blended suspension had long-tailed absorption. It might be resulted from the geometrical feature of the CNF. The fibrous structure in the CNF scattered the light, as shown in Data in Brief (Fig. 4 (Ahn et al., 2020)). The scattering by the CNF hindered the energy radiation to the CD. Considering the volumetric difference of the CNF from the CD, the scattering by the CNF would be dominant in the suspension. The CD@CNF suspension exhibited a broad and fast decreasing absorption curve. Compared with the blended suspension, the scattering effect was trivial because most of the CDs were located on the CNF surface. The CD of CD@CNF absorbed the energy in the wavelength shorter than 400 nm without a significant peak. The diverse functional groups of the CD generated the energy level spectrum under the π * level. The scattering by the CNF was also observed over 400 nm, resulting in a tailed absorption curve as like the CD-CNF suspension.

Fig. 4 (b)–(d) show the PL emission spectra of each sample. The emission spectrum of the CD suspension had a peak at 438 nm. The emission wavelength of the CD had weak excitation wavelength dependency. It implies that the CD was not fully graphitized and most of the emissions were originated from the recombination of the energy levels in the functional groups (Ahn, Song, Kwon, Lee et al., 2019). In the CD-CNF blend suspension, the emission was slightly blue-shifted (∼5 nm) at all excitation wavelength, compared to the CD suspension. The interactions between the functional groups of the CD and the CNF changed the recombination pathways. Compared with the blended suspension, the CD@CNF suspension had a similar emission spectrum but a broader peak at 433 nm. The broad peaks were aroused from the chemical linkage between the CD and the CNF. The diverse surface states of the CD in CD@CNF split the surface energy levels more finely in the wide range. Moreover, the CD@CNF suspension showed strong emission maxima wavelength dependency on the excitation wavelength. The energy level was more finely split below the π * energy level with narrow intervals because of the diverse surface states of the CD in CD@CNF.

Thermogravimetric analysis was conducted to study the thermal decomposition behavior of the samples. As displayed in Fig. 5 (a), the CD exhibited three DTG peaks. The degradation peak at 160 °C was driven by the functional groups of the CDs such as amine groups (Pletincx et al., 2017). The DTG peaks at 242 and 358 °C came from the degradation of the core. The poor graphitic structure in the core led the degradation at lower temperature than typical graphene quantum dots (Konwar, Gogoi, Majumdar, & Chowdhury, 2015). The CNF film had two DTG peaks at 243 and 310 °C (Suenaga & Osada, 2018). The degradation at lower temperature was related to the degradation of the carboxylated glucose group, and the degradation at higher temperature was assigned to the degradation of the cellulose chain, respectively.

The embedded CD changed the thermal degradation behaviors of the CD and the CNF, regardless of the embedding methods, i.e., blend or in-situ synthesis. Both the CD-embedded films had degradation peak around 190 °C, which was originated from the degradation of the CD. Comparing to the CD, the CD in the films was decomposed in higher temperature. The interaction between the CDs and the CNF enhanced the thermal stability of the CDs in both blended and in-situ synthesized films. The CDs in the CD-CNF and the CD@CNF films interacted with the CNF by secondary and primary bondings, respectively. In the case of CD-CNF, the surface of the CD was covered by the CNF and the degradation of functional groups would be hindered. The coverage effect of CNF can be found also in Data in Brief (Fig. 5 of (Ahn et al., 2020)). As the lower contents of the CD included, the higher degradation temperature was observed. In other words, more functional groups of the CD could be covered by the CNF and hindered from degradation. In the case of CD@CNF, the increase of the degradation temperature can be contributed to the new chemical bonding, as well known. The new chemical linkage could enhance the thermal stability of materials. As similar to the CD, the thermal behaviors of the CNF in the films were significantly altered. The degradation temperatures for the glucuronate groups and the cellulose chain were elevated toward higher temperature and the peak intensity of the glucuronate group degradation was decreased, compared to the cellulose chain degradation peak. Interestingly, the thermal behaviors of the films did not show significant difference according to the binding states of the CD to the CNF. The CD increased the thermal stability of the CNF. As discussed in Data in Brief (Fig. 5 of (Ahn et al., 2020)), the thermal stability was mainly dependent on the CD content. Given the similar thermal degradation behavior of each film, it can be assumed that the CD contents in each film were not significantly different.

Fig. 6 shows the hydrophilic feature and the swelling behavior of the CNF, according to the CD embedment. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), the CNF showed low water contact angle (WCA), because of the huge amount of hydroxyl groups. After embedding the CD in the CNF by either blending or in-situ synthesis, the WCA was dramatically increased. The CD had relatively hydrophobic feature driven from its aromatic carbon structure. The CD@CNF film showed elevated WCA than the blended film. The difference was originated from the anchored state of the CD to the CNF. The CD in the blended film would partially aggregate each other (Fig. 6 (a) of (Ahn et al., 2020)). On the contrast, the in-situ synthesized CD was developed in the form of enveloping the CNF, without aggregation.

Fig. 6 (b)–(d) illustrate the swelling behaviors of the prepared films in water. As expected, the pristine CNF film showed excellent swelling behaviors compared to the other films. It was largely swollen by water due to the huge amounts of functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxylate groups. Moreover, the ionized carboxylate groups in the CNF electrostatically repelled each other in water and made enough room for water uptake. The addition of CD eventually changed the swelling behaviors of the films. As described in the FT-IR and the XPS, most of the ionized carboxylate groups were linked with the CD or converted to carboxyl/amide groups. The change from ionic bonding to covalent bonding of the carboxylate group reduced the ionic repulsion between the CNFs. Instead, it led the strong inter/intramolecular hydrogen bonding in solid-state. When the bonding was formed, it was hard to dissociate even in water, weakening water uptake capability. The hydrogen bonding also aggregated the nanofibers, which was challenging to be nano-fibrillated again for the same reason (Wang et al., 2019). As a result, the films containing CDs were less swollen than the pristine CNF film. Comparing to the CD blended film, the CD@CNF film showed less swelling and better dimensional stability. It is noticeable that CD@CNF successfully maintained its own shape even after one month of immersion in water due to its hydrophobic feature and stable fixation of the CD on the fiber surface. When the CD was blended with the CNF, the CD was dangled to the CNF by physical interaction or secondary bonding, such as van der Waals attraction or hydrogen bonding. The physically attached CD can be loosened and detached from the CNF by long term immersion in water. However, the in-situ synthesized CD was firmly attached and enveloped on the fiber surface mainly by covalent bonding. The CD was not loosened or released from CD@CNF, even if they were immersed in water for a long time. As a result, the CD@CNF film showed outstanding long-term dimensional stability. The CD-envelopment enhanced the morphological stability against chemical reaction, as shown in Data in Brief (Fig. 6 (b) of (Ahn et al., 2020)). As already recognized, CNF has high chemical reactivity. In the presence of hydroxyl radical, the fully swollen CNF film lost its shape very fast. The CD embedded films maintained their shape stably for 1 h. It can be considered as the CD increased the chemical resistance of CNF by blocking the carboxylate group. Compared with the CD-CNF blended film, the CD@CNF film showed better dimensional stability. Due to the low interaction between the CD and the CNF, the CD-CNF film was ruptured from inside to outside. By the effect of CD-envelopment, however, CD@CNF maintained its shape for 3 h.

Those dimensional stability of CD@CNF extends its applications toward water treatment industries. Fig. 7 displays the water filtration performance of the CD@CNF membrane. For the test, CD@CNF with different loading amounts (10, 20 and 30 gsm) was prepared and compared with the pristine CNF membrane (Fig. 8 (a) of (Ahn et al., 2020) & Fig. 7 (a)). The CD@CNF membrane with 10 gsm was burst and disrupted during the test due to its inferior mechanical properties. Under the same loading amounts, the CD@CNF membrane had almost 3 times higher pure water flux compared to the CNF (Fig. 7 (a)). The remarkable increase of pure water flux can be explained as follows. As mentioned in the TEM images and the SAXS analysis, the fiber diameter was slightly increased via the in-situ synthesis. Moreover, the surface of the fibrous structure of CD@CNF became rough and bumpy. Under the water pressure, nanofibrous structure was compressed and then closed the pores in the membrane. The CD on the CNF played a similar role as a spacer and resisted against the compaction. It gave more space for water penetration. Secondly, the introduction of the CD on the CNF surface controlled the interaction between the fibers. As described in Fig. 6, the morphological durability of the CD@CNF membrane was originated from the chemical linkage between the CD and the CNF. The carboxylate groups of the CNF repelled each other severely and led to easy swelling by water. On the other hand, the CD in CD@CNF blocked most of the carboxylate groups and reduced the repulsion. This ensures the easy transport of water through the pore. The CD@CNF membrane showed excellent durability under water pressure, as shown in Data in Brief (Fig. 8 (b) of (Ahn et al., 2020)). Under the pressure, the water permeability was maintained for 48 h. The SEM images of the membranes were also displayed in Data in Brief (Fig. 7 of (Ahn et al., 2020)). In the surface images, each membrane showed a smooth surface without any noticeable defects. Unfortunately, the pores in the membrane were not observed because of their micro∼meso size. The higher magnification images of the CD@CNF membrane showed the significant increase of the fiber diameter, which was confirmed by the SAXS and the AFM analyses.

The CD@CNF membrane showed excellent rejection performance against cationic dyes. The MB rejection rate of the membrane exceeded 99 % regardless of the loading amounts (Fig. 7 (a)). For the cycle test, the membrane was washed with 50 mL of EtOH before the sequential rejection test (Fig. 9 (a) of (Ahn et al., 2020 & Fig. 7 (b))). The CD@CNF membrane showed excellent performance of reusability up to the third cycle having a 95 % rejection. The high rejection rate with high flux was granted by the CD on the fibers surface. As well known, the cationic dye can highly interact with carboxylate groups due to its negative charge. The zeta potential of the CNF suspension was −59 eV while that of the CD@CNF suspension was −36 eV. This indicates the significant amount of the carboxylate groups were removed or blocked. Despite of the loss of the negative charge, the composite has still good affinity to MB because the functional groups and the graphitized structure of the CD could also interact with MB (Li, Guo, Lv, & Fan, 2019). The remained carboxylate groups of the CNF and the functional groups of the CD together could synergistically reject the cationic dyes successfully.

For further application, dyes with different charge were tested (Fig. 7 (c) and Table 1 ). As expected, the CD@CNF membrane showed excellent selectivity on cationic dye rejection. MB and JGB were mostly rejected (99.9 %) regardless of their molecular size. Meanwhile, the rejection rates of anionic dyes (MO and OG) were lower than 10 %, even though the molecular size of MO and MB are similar. The slightly higher rejection rate against VB12 (neutral dye) might be due to the larger molecular size of VB12 than MO and OG. Even in the mixture of three different charged dyes (VB12, OG and MB), the CD@CNF membrane rejected most of MB (99.9 %) and only rejected OG and VB12 less than 25 %. Through the test, it could be inferred the rejection mechanism of the membrane primarily follows the charge interactions between CD@CNF and the dyes. The selective cationic dye removal performance in the mixture of dyes was superior to the CNF membrane (Fig. 8 (b) of (Ahn et al., 2020) & Fig. 7 (c)). The CNF membrane intrinsically have cationic dye removal capacity owing to its carboxylate groups, as mentioned above. However, the rejection rate of MB was only 96 % in the presence of anionic and neutral dyes. CD@CNF showed good dye selectivity regardless of their loading amounts (Fig. 8 (c) of (Ahn et al., 2020)). The in-situ synthesized CD@CNF composite has great potential of high-performance cationic dye removal membrane.

In the study, the CD was anchored and enveloped on the CNF by one-step hydrothermal treatment. The CD was successfully in-situ synthesized on the CNF surface, without aggregate itself. Also, the process condition did not significantly change the fibrous structure of the CNF. The CD in CD@CNF was chemically connected to the CNF by amide bonding. Moreover, the homogeneously dispersed CD on the CNF surface gave the CD@CNF film extraordinary dimensional stability in aqueous solution. Using the features, CD@CNF was applied to the dye rejection membrane. Without any help of extra spacer, the CD@CNF membrane showed pure water flux over 30 LMH bar−1 with methylene blue rejection rate over 99 %. The membrane also showed selective dye removal performance. The CD@CNF membrane revealed excellent potential in water treatment.

Jungbin Ahn: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Sewon Pak: Validation. Younghan Song: Conceptualization. Hyungsup Kim: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science (Grant No. 2020R1A6A3A13075205 & 2020R1I1A2069032).
